A kangaroo can hop at speeds up to 56 km/h (35 mph) — faster than a horse!
Baby kangaroos (joeys) are born the size of a jellybean and crawl into the pouch.
Kangaroos can't walk backwards — that's why they're on Australia's coat of arms (always moving forward).
A group of kangaroos is called a 'mob'. Some mobs have over 100 members!
Red kangaroos are the largest marsupials on Earth, standing up to 1.8m (6 ft) tall.
Kangaroos use their thick tail as a 'fifth leg' for balance and even as a tripod when standing.
Female kangaroos can pause a pregnancy if conditions aren't right — it's called embryonic diapause.
There are about 45 million kangaroos in Australia — nearly twice the human population!
Kangaroos are excellent swimmers and will sometimes flee into water to escape predators.
A kangaroo's hop is incredibly energy-efficient — they use less energy the faster they go.
Tree-kangaroos exist! They live in tropical rainforests and are excellent climbers.
Kangaroos have been on Earth for about 15–20 million years.
An average kangaroo hop covers about 8 metres (26 feet) in a single bound.
